bibliographic.citation: Reginald Pecock, Reginald Pecock's Book of Faith: A Fifteenth Century Theological Tractate, edited from the MS. in the Library of Trinity College, Cambridge, with an introductory essay by J. L. Morison (Glasgow: James MacLehose and Sons, 1909).
bibliographic.title: Reginald Pecock's Book of Faith: A Fifteenth Century Theological Tractate

entities.label: Morison's 1909 edition of The Book of Faith
notes: The complete surviving medieval text occupies printed pp. 109–305. Morison's preface, introductory essay, summary, table of authorities, glossary, and indexes are distinct editorial matter.

Morison says the first object was a complete text; manuscript spelling was retained except for stated obvious absurdity and a symbol substitution, while punctuation is entirely editorial and a few bare textual explanations are supplied.
Morison reports the manuscript as 127 folios and repeats an older early-fifteenth-century dating. The current Trinity catalog instead records 126 folios and only the fifteenth century.

rights.basis: The volume was first published in 1909, well before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff for published works.
rights.component: Morison's 1909 edited text, introduction, annotations, and arrangement
notes: This U.S. conclusion does not itself determine every non-U.S. jurisdiction or rights in modern scans.

access.accessMethod: For publication or reuse beyond the item license, email the Wren Library or write care of the Digital Services Manager and describe the Git repository and commercial/POD context.
notes: Already digitized images are downloadable without an image-supply charge for personal study, but Trinity asks researchers to apply for publication permission and says prices vary by intended use.

dateStatements.label: current institutional dating
dateStatements.notes: The normalized lower bound reflects the work's c.1456–57 composition within the catalog's broad fifteenth-century date; no narrower paleographical range is asserted.

entities.description: The sole known manuscript witness of The Book of Faith, a fifteenth-century parchment codex written 22 lines to a page. It lacks two leaves in quire 2 and all remaining material after current f.126v, so the text is internally lacunose and ends imperfectly.

holding.notes: Given by Archbishop John Whitgift. A flyleaf title calls the contents Reginald Pecock's sermons in English and marks them imperfect; a modern pencil note names Stowe.

entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge MS B.14.45
notes: Current fols.1r–126v and Morison's legacy 1a–129b milestones describe the same witness. They remain separate locator systems pending a complete crosswalk.
The current catalog explicitly calls B.14.45 the only known manuscript of the treatise.

physicalDescription.extent: 126 folios in the current Trinity catalog
notes: Twenty-two lines to a page; the catalog calls it well written.
Collation: 1^8 2^8 (wants 4, 5) 3^8–16^8, rest gone.
The prologue ends on f.8; Part I has ten chapters and ends on f.79; Part II begins on f.79v, has five surviving chapters, and ends imperfectly on f.126v.
Marginal notes occur in a small fifteenth-century hand, with additions in lower margins by the original hand.

rights.basis: The underlying medieval text predates modern copyright by centuries. Physical ownership, access control, modern catalog text, and manuscript photography remain separate layers.
rights.component: Fifteenth-century Middle English and Latin textual content embodied in the physical codex

bibliographic.citation: Reginald Pecock, A Treatise Proving Scripture to Be the Rule of Faith, writ by Reginald Peacock ... before the Reformation, about the year MCDL, edited with a preface and glossary by Henry Wharton (London: printed for James Adamson, 1688).
bibliographic.title: A Treatise Proving Scripture to Be the Rule of Faith

entities.label: Wharton's 1688 partial edition of The Book of Faith
notes: Wharton says he transcribed B.14.45 directly. He omitted the prologue and most of Part I as too obscure, printed selected Part I fragments, and printed Part II complete as far as the imperfect manuscript allowed.
The publication is an actual partial historical transmission of Pecock's work, not merely later commentary.

rights.basis: The work was published in 1688 and predates modern copyright.
rights.component: Wharton's 1688 preface, partial transcription, glossary, and printed arrangement

next_steps: Seek a path-specific licensing or provenance statement from the CME repository maintainers.
Check upstream U-M terms and repository history for an explicit grant covering the imported XML serialization and any later repository additions.
Until then, retain unknown artifact-level status and do not transfer the root software license to the U-M encoding, Morison edition, page images, or other upstream layers.
openQuestions.question: Does the pinned CME repository's root Unlicense expressly cover the inherited U-M XML data, transcription, encoding, and markup at CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/ajh1649.xml?

next_steps: Collate all 251 XML milestones against the 252 current folio images while preserving the two missing leaves in quire 2 as physical lacunae.

Test whether the two missing quire-2 leaves explain two of the three folio numbers between legacy 129b and current 126v, and do not force the one further discrepancy into an assumed renumbering without inspecting every anomaly.
notes: The two systems describe one witness; they are not separate witnesses.
openQuestions.question: How does Morison's legacy 1a–129b foliation map surface by surface to Trinity's current f.1r–126v IIIF sequence?

next_steps: Compare internal cross-references, related Pecock works, Wharton's selection, and current scholarship.
Retain Morison's estimate of at least two lost chapters as an attributed minimum rather than a recovered original total.
openQuestions.question: How much text and how many original chapters were lost after current f.126v?

next_steps: Ask U-M for the complete image bundle and item-level production record.
Ask whether No Copyright–United States applies to both the OCR/XML and TIFF delivery layer despite the generic 2006 restriction warning.
openQuestions.question: Can U-M provide a stable complete download of the 316 TIFFs and clarify the exact rights scope for OCR/XML versus page images?

next_steps: Email wren.library@trin.cam.ac.uk with the exact Git/POD use case and request written permission beyond CC BY-NC where necessary.
Keep the complete cache private and gitignored unless the provider license and any additional permission clearly authorize redistribution.
openQuestions.question: Will Trinity authorize redistribution of the complete B.14.45 image bundle in a repository supporting commercial and noncommercial print-on-demand publication?

next_steps: Consult recent Pecock chronology and textual scholarship before assigning London or another settlement as a composition place.
openQuestions.question: Can the work's exact place of composition be distinguished from Pecock's residence and the mainly London audience argued in scholarship?

relations.subjectLabel: Kelly's 2021 study of the judicial processes against Pecock

reviewNotes: The primary transmission path is repository artifact → U-M OCR encoding → U-M page-image set → Morison 1909 edition → Trinity B.14.45. The independent Internet Archive scan and complete Trinity IIIF object are supporting facsimile routes.

Wharton 1688 is a passage-scoped historical intermediary: it directly transcribes selected Part I material and the surviving Part II from B.14.45. Morison's relation to it is limited to explicit selected apparatus comparisons; B.14.45 remains Morison's continuous source.
The 1688 print expression, its subscription-gated EEBO source images, and the public CC0 EEBO-TCP encoding are separate entities with separate rights and access statements.
The current 126-folio Trinity inventory and Morison/U-M 1a–129b milestones remain parallel. The manifest does not invent a second witness or a forced exact crosswalk.

Every cached object is complete relative to its provider source: the full Morison volume, complete Internet Archive item-metadata JSON, full A53931 XML, full Kelly article, complete four-page Copyright Office circular, and all 260 Trinity canvases. Only the Trinity cache is a complete physical-manuscript surrogate, and the manuscript itself remains textually incomplete.

Kelly's article is provisionally typed catalog_description because schema 1.0.0 has no dedicated scholarship or study entity type; its PDF is modeled as a separate delivery facsimile and does not enter the textual transmission path.

The repository artifact's root Unlicense evidence, U-M's current No Copyright–United States assertion, and the historical source layers remain separate. The software-worded root license is not treated as blanket permission for inherited U-M XML or later repository serialization additions.

lineage.summary: The checked-in ajh1649 XML is a local representation of University of Michigan CME item AJH1649.0001.001, an uncorrected 2006 OCR linked to 316 page images of J. L. Morison's 1909 edition. Morison's first objective was a complete text, transcribed directly from the sole known witness, Trinity College Cambridge MS B.14.45; he retained manuscript spelling subject to stated exceptions, supplied all punctuation, and used Henry Wharton's 1688 partial transcript only for isolated comparisons rather than as his continuous base. Wharton independently transcribed selected Part I excerpts and the surviving Part II from B.14.45, and that print was later encoded as EEBO-TCP A53931. Complete public local caches now preserve the Morison scan, the EEBO-TCP XML, and all 260 canvases of Trinity's B.14.45 IIIF object. Current Trinity foliation 1r–126v and Morison/U-M legacy milestones 1a–129b remain parallel because an exact surface crosswalk is unresolved.

catalogSummary.author: Reginald Pecock
catalogSummary.date: Composed c. 1456–1457; attested in the sole known fifteenth-century manuscript; partially printed 1688 and first completely printed from the surviving text in 1909
catalogSummary.region: England; exact place of composition unresolved
catalogSummary.title: The Book of Faith

catalogingSubject.label: The Book of Faith
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Reginald Pecock's single Middle English prose work surviving incompletely in Trinity College Cambridge MS B.14.45: a prologue, ten chapters of Part I, and five chapters of Part II. This subject excludes J. L. Morison's 1909 introduction, summary, punctuation and notes as editorial matter; Henry Wharton's partial 1688 publication; the University of Michigan OCR and page images; the physical codex and its modern photographs; and the checked-in repository XML as distinct transmission layers.

contentParts.label: Prologue
contentParts.summary: Diagnoses lay disobedience and reliance on private biblical interpretation, rejects clerical infallibility as an effective ground of persuasion, proposes obedience to fallible teachers where error has not been proved, and presents vernacular apologetic writing as a means of engaging dissenters.

contentParts.label: Part I
notes: The gap in quire 2 removes the end of chapter 1 and beginning of chapter 2. The XML's legacy foliation skips 12 and 13; the current Trinity sequence remains 126 physical folios.
contentParts.summary: Ten chapters define faith and its relation to reason and evidence, reject accounts that make faith an unsupported act of will or private illumination, and argue for obedience to the church despite its possible fallibility. Chapter 8 directly confronts a Lollard interlocutor; the section closes by insisting that private dissent must prove ecclesiastical error in public disputation.

contentParts.label: Part II
notes: The explicit is absent because the sole witness ends imperfectly after ‘and also thei overleepen this article.’
contentParts.summary: Five surviving chapters examine whether church or Scripture is the chief ground of faith. Pecock treats oral tradition, apostolic preaching and writing, the compilation of Genesis, scriptural interpretation, apostolic succession, the Apostles' Creed, and the church's inability to create major new articles of faith. The manuscript breaks off during chapter 5.

assertion.notes: Kelly dates the work c. 1456–57; Morison argues for about 1456. The range is approximate, not a dated colophon.
The current institutional catalog dates the codex only to the fifteenth century; the lower bound is inferred from the independently supported composition date.

dateStatements.display: Sole known witness: after the work's c. 1456–1457 composition and within the fifteenth century
notes: Morison repeats the older James catalog's ‘early fifteenth century’ dating, which cannot describe a copy of a work composed around 1456; it is preserved as a historical conflict rather than normalized.

scope.description: Part I excerpts and the surviving Part II as selected by Henry Wharton

assertion.notes: Morison states that a complete text was his first object and characterizes Wharton's 1688 publication as partial.

dateStatements.display: 1909 (first complete printed edition of the surviving text)

scope.description: All text surviving in B.14.45, not the work's lost leaves or ending

extent.description: The extant structure is one prologue, ten Part I chapter containers, and five Part II chapter containers. Within BODY the XML contains 198 PB, 251 folio MILESTONE, 111 P, and 89 NOTE1 elements, with no L or LG elements. The witness lacks two leaves in quire 2 and all material after the abrupt end at current f.126v, so 15 surviving chapters are not a certain count of the originally composed whole.

formStatements.description: A prose theological dialogue between a father and son; in Part I chapter 8 the son is temporarily displaced by a Lollard interlocutor and the dialogue becomes more hortatory.

assertion.notes: The work argues against lay and Lollard resistance to clerical authority while also rejecting a simple appeal to ecclesiastical infallibility.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The U-M header's generic eng code describes the bibliographic item without distinguishing Pecock's Middle English from Morison's modern English editorial matter.

scope.description: Biblical, patristic, doctrinal, and scholastic quotations and terms embedded in the Middle English argument

notes: The current Trinity catalog's 126-folio inventory supersedes Morison's 127-folio physical count for present-day access, while Morison's 1a–129b milestones remain necessary locators for the 1909 edition and U-M encoding.

Wharton 1688 belongs to historical transmission: it directly transcribes selected material from B.14.45. It is not Morison's continuous base, though Morison reports at least one selected Wharton reading in his apparatus.

The complete Trinity digital object covers all 260 provider canvases, including covers and flyleaves; the target work occupies the internally incomplete manuscript text on current fols. 1r–126v, canvases 5–256.

next_steps: Consult current Pecock chronology and manuscript studies that distinguish intended audience, residence, drafting, revision, and circulation.
Do not convert an argued London audience into a place-of-composition claim without direct evidence.
openQuestions.question: Can the composition date and place be established more narrowly than c. 1456–1457 in England?

next_steps: Retain both systems in parallel and collate the 251 XML milestones against all 252 current folio images.

Test whether the two physically missing leaves in quire 2 explain two of the three folio numbers between legacy 129b and current 126v, and resolve the one further discrepancy before publishing a crosswalk.
notes: The XML has no milestones 12a–13b and ends at 129b; the current institutional inventory has 126 folios. These are competing locator systems for one witness, not evidence of multiple manuscripts.
openQuestions.question: How does Morison's legacy 1a–129b foliation map surface by surface to Trinity's current f.1r–126v image sequence?

next_steps: Compare internal cross-references, Wharton's excerpts, Pecock's related works, and modern scholarship without treating Morison's estimate of at least two lost chapters as a complete original count.
openQuestions.question: How many chapters and how much text were lost after the abrupt end of Part II chapter 5?

next_steps: Ask U-M for the item-level image bundle, OCR production record, and clarification of whether the current No Copyright–United States statement covers both OCR/XML and linked TIFFs.
Preserve the XML's generic 2006 distribution warning alongside the newer collection-level rights statement until clarified.
openQuestions.question: Can U-M supply an exact downloadable bundle and production log for the 316 TIFF page images and 2006 uncorrected OCR?

next_steps: Request written publication and repository-reuse permission from wren.library@trin.cam.ac.uk, explicitly describing the Git repository and commercial/POD context.
Keep the complete IIIF cache private and gitignored unless the CC BY-NC terms and any additional Trinity permission clearly cover the proposed redistribution.
openQuestions.question: Will Trinity authorize redistribution of the B.14.45 photographs in a repository that supports commercial and noncommercial print-on-demand publication?

notes: England is the work's linguistic, ecclesiastical, and intended cultural setting. Kelly reports an argument that Pecock wrote mainly for a London audience, but audience does not establish the exact place of composition.

scope.description: The 1909 scholarly edition that immediately underlies the U-M item

assertion.notes: Morison's preface makes a complete text his first objective and explicitly states his treatment of manuscript spelling, punctuation, symbols, and textual explanations.

scope.description: The complete surviving medieval text on Morison's printed pp. 109–305

assertion.notes: At printed p. 236 Morison expressly reports a Wharton reading; the present evidence does not support a claim of systematic full-text collation.

scope.description: Isolated apparatus comparisons with Wharton's 1688 partial transcript, not a continuous base text
Wharton's 1688 edition of selected Part I excerpts and the complete surviving Part II available to him
Selected extracts transcribed directly from the Trinity manuscript

subjects.label: The Apostles' Creed and lay religious instruction

summaries.value: A late Middle English theological dialogue in which Pecock seeks a rational way to restore lay dissenters to obedience without grounding that obedience in ecclesiastical infallibility. The prologue frames the work as a response to English lay readers who privilege their own reading of Scripture over clerical teaching. Part I defines faith, argues that evidence and reason strengthen rather than destroy it, and maintains that Christians owe obedience to the church unless its error can be publicly and irrefutably proved. Part II tests the relation between church and Scripture through arguments about oral tradition, apostolic teaching, interpretation, the Apostles' Creed, and whether the clergy can create new articles of faith. The sole manuscript loses two leaves internally and breaks off during Part II chapter 5.

notes: This is the established modern title used by the current holding institution and scholarship; the manuscript item itself has no heading.

titles.value: Reginald Pecock's Book of Faith: A Fifteenth Century Theological Tractate

assertion.notes: Wharton says the author entitled the whole work A Treatise of Faith, but the current Trinity catalog says that the surviving manuscript item has no heading.

notes: Retained as an attributed authorial title, not as a rubric directly visible at the present opening of B.14.45.
The incipit combines a Latin quotation from Lamentations with Pecock's Middle English rendering.
titles.value: Facti sunt filii mei perditi, quia invaluit inimicus: that is to seie in Englisch thus: My sones ben maad lost for the enemye hath had the maistrie

subjects.label: The Apostles' Creed and lay religious instruction

work.summary: A late Middle English theological dialogue in which Pecock seeks a rational way to restore lay dissenters to obedience without grounding that obedience in ecclesiastical infallibility. The prologue frames the work as a response to English lay readers who privilege their own reading of Scripture over clerical teaching. Part I defines faith, argues that evidence and reason strengthen rather than destroy it, and maintains that Christians owe obedience to the church unless its error can be publicly and irrefutably proved. Part II tests the relation between church and Scripture through arguments about oral tradition, apostolic teaching, interpretation, the Apostles' Creed, and whether the clergy can create new articles of faith. The sole manuscript loses two leaves internally and breaks off during Part II chapter 5.
